answer:
Sketch:
- Exactly-once *delivery* impossible (Two Generals; network/crash ambiguity)
- Exactly-once *processing* achievable via idempotency
- At-least-once + idempotent consumers / dedup keys
- Transactional outbox / Kafka "exactly-once semantics" = atomic offsets+writes, still bounded scope

Answer: No — exactly-once *delivery* is theoretically impossible in a distributed system: if an ack is lost, the sender can't know whether the message arrived, so it must either redeliver (at-least-once) or not (at-most-once). Your teammate is likely thinking of exactly-once *processing*, which is achievable and is the standard approach: keep at-least-once delivery and make consumers idempotent — attach a unique message/idempotency key, have the consumer record processed keys (ideally in the same transaction as its side effects) and skip duplicates. Complement with a transactional outbox on the producer side to avoid dropped/phantom sends. Systems marketed as "exactly-once" (e.g., Kafka EOS) do exactly this internally — atomic offset commits plus dedup — and only within their own boundary; once you touch an external system, idempotency is on you.
